A statement like `String dest = origin` creates a copy of object `origin` and assigns that copy to the variable `dest`.

childMdx.rawBody:
An assignment of a reference to a variable copies the **reference**,
not the object the reference points to.

In Java, a variable can only ever store a **reference** to an object.
Variables in Java cannot store an object itself.
So if you copy the value of one variable into another variable,
you copy the reference, not the object.

So, for example:

```java
Pacman p1 = new Pacman();
Pacman p2 = p1;
```

The first line of the above code creates a `Pacman` object
and assigns a reference to that object to variable `p1`.
The second line copies the **reference** that is stored in `p1` into variable `p2`.
At the end of this code, there still is only a single `Pacman` object.
And there are two variables, `p1` and `p2` that both point to that same object.

This can provide an opportunity to discuss value types (inline classes),
which require one to give up on identity.

frontmatter.shortCorrection: Assignment copies the reference pointing to the object
